english: Leit (leading) + Faden (thread). A 'leading thread' through complexity. German companies produce Leitfaeden for everything. Ariadne's thread through the labyrinth of process. Related to 'roter Faden' (common thread).
Neuter noun. Praxis (practice) + Beispiel (example).\nTheory brought to life through real cases.

Praxis (practice) + Beispiel (example). German presentations love Praxisbeispiele. Theory without practice is suspect. 'Können Sie ein Praxisbeispiel nennen?' is a frequent audience question that separates good speakers from great ones.
Feminine noun. Schluss (end) + Folgerung (inference).\n'Daraus ergibt sich die Schlussfolgerung...'

Schluss (conclusion) + Folgerung (inference). The logical endpoint of an argument. 'Daraus ergibt sich die Schlussfolgerung...' (From this follows the conclusion...) is academic gold. Every schluessiges Argument leads to a Schlussfolgerung.
Feminine noun. Gruss (greeting) + Formel (formula).\nThe opening and closing phrases of a Vortrag.

Gruss (greeting) + Formel (formula). In German presentations, the opening Grussformel sets the tone. 'Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren' (formal) versus 'Liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen' (warm professional). Choose wrong and you lose the room.
Feminine noun. From French 'pointe' (point/tip).\nStress on the second syllable: po-IN-te.

From French 'pointe' (point/tip). 'Eine gute Pointe setzen' (to deliver a good punchline). In German, the stress falls on the second syllable: po-IN-te. A Vortrag with a Pointe is memorable. Without one, it is just data.
From inter (between) + aktiv (active).\n'Ein interaktiver Workshop.'

Inter (between) + aktiv (active). 'Ein interaktiver Vortrag' involves the audience. German presentation culture is moving toward more interaktive Formate. Questions during the talk, not just after. The audience becomes part of the event.
Praxis + nah (close/near).\n'Praxisnahe Lösungen' are what clients demand.

Praxis + nah (close/near). Close to real-world application. The opposite of 'theoretisch.' 'Praxisnahe Lösungen' (practical solutions) is what German clients demand. Abstract ideas must be brought nah an die Praxis.
From schliessen (to close/conclude).\n'Ein schluessiges Argument' has no gaps.

From 'schliessen' (to close/conclude). Everything 'closes' together without gaps. Your reasoning must be lueckenlos (without gaps) to be schluessig. Mia's highest compliment for Verumius's presentation structure.
From treffen (to hit/meet).\n'Eine treffende Formulierung' hits the mark.

From 'treffen' (to hit/meet). Like an arrow: your words treffend means they land exactly where intended. 'Eine treffende Bemerkung' (an apt remark) shows you understand the core of the matter. Mia uses this word when Verumius nails it.
Stich (stab/poke) + haltig (holding).\n'Ein stichhaltiges Argument' survives testing.

Stich (stab/poke) + haltig (holding). An argument that 'holds up when poked at.' It survives testing. The ultimate compliment for a B2-level argument. If your reasoning is stichhaltig, no Einwand can topple it.
Fach (field) + kundig (knowledgeable).\n'Fachkundig beraten' (to advise expertly).

Fach (field) + kundig (knowledgeable). Having deep knowledge in your field. 'Fachkundiges Publikum' means your audience knows the subject too. When the audience is fachkundig, you cannot hide behind jargon.
Stichwort (keyword) + artig (like).\n'Stichwortartig zusammenfassen' is efficient.

Stichwort (keyword/cue) + artig (like). How Germans take notes: no full sentences, just Stichworte. Efficient and focused. 'Stichwortartig notieren' (to note in keywords) is the standard method for Mitschriften in meetings.
Not separable. From gaenzen (to complete, archaic).\n'Darf ich etwas ergänzen?' is polite meeting rhetoric.

From 'gaenzen' (to complete, archaic). Not separable. 'Darf ich etwas ergänzen?' (May I add something?) is the polite way to expand on someone's point in a meeting. You do not contradict, you ergänzen.
Not separable. Be- + kraeftigen (to strengthen).\nStronger than zustimmen (to agree).

Be- + kraeftigen (to strengthen). You actively strengthen a claim. Stronger than 'zustimmen' (to agree). 'Ich möchte diese Aussage bekraeftigen' (I would like to reinforce this statement) shows you are not just nodding along.
Not separable. Ver- + anschaulich (vivid) + -en.\n'Ein Diagramm veranschaulicht die Daten.'

Ver- + anschaulich (vivid) + -en. Making something visible. 'Ein Diagramm veranschaulicht die Daten' (A diagram illustrates the data). The verb form of 'anschaulich.' Verumius's aqueduct analogy is pure Veranschaulichung.
Not separable. Unter (under) + streichen (to draw/stroke).\nStrong verb: unterstrich, unterstrichen.

Unter (under) + streichen (to stroke/draw). Drawing a line under something to make it stand out. Not separable in this meaning. Strong verb: unterstreichen/unterstrich/unterstrichen. The metaphor of marking importance with a line.
Separable: nahe + legen. Die Daten legen nahe, dass...\nMore elegant than vorschlagen.

Nahe (near) + legen (to lay). Separable. You 'lay something near' someone for them to consider. More elegant than 'vorschlagen.' 'Die Daten legen nahe, dass...' (The data suggest that...) is scientific register at its finest.
Not separable. From Latin praecisus (exact).\n'Können Sie das präzisieren?' is a common meeting question.

From Latin 'praecisus' (cut short/exact). Not separable. 'Können Sie das präzisieren?' (Can you be more specific?) is a common German meeting question. Precision is not pedantry in German culture. It is respect.
Neuter noun. From Greek protokollon (first page of a scroll).\n'Wer führt Protokoll?' starts every German meeting.

From Greek 'protokollon' (first page glued to a scroll). In German meetings, someone always writes the Protokoll. 'Wer führt Protokoll?' (Who is taking minutes?) is asked at the start of every meeting. No meeting is complete without one.
Feminine noun. Rueck (back) + Meldung (report).\nThe German alternative to the English loanword 'Feedback.'

Rueck (back) + Meldung (report). The German alternative to the English loanword 'Feedback.' 'Haben Sie eine Rueckmeldung?' (Do you have feedback?) is more formal than 'Haben Sie Feedback?' Both are used, but Rueckmeldung is purer German.
Feminine noun. An (at) + Merkung (noting).\n'Ich hätte eine Anmerkung.' Lighter than Einwand.

An (at) + Merkung (noting). 'Ich hätte eine Anmerkung' (I would have a remark) is the polite way to comment during a presentation. Lighter than an Einwand (objection). You note something without attacking the speaker.
Feminine noun. From erläutern (to explain).\n'Zur Erläuterung...' introduces context.

From 'erläutern' (to explain/elucidate). More thorough than a brief Erklärung. 'Zur Erläuterung...' (For clarification...) introduces additional context in presentations. A good Erläuterung makes the complex accessible.
Feminine noun. Mit (along) + Schrift (writing).\nLess official than Protokoll, more personal.

Mit (along) + Schrift (writing). Writing along with the speaker. 'Eine Mitschrift anfertigen' (to take notes). Less official than a Protokoll, more personal. Your Mitschrift is your version of what was said.
Feminine noun. Ab + Stimmung (from Stimme = voice).\n'Wir kommen zur Abstimmung.'

Ab + Stimmung (from Stimme = voice). 'Wir kommen zur Abstimmung' (We will now vote). Each person's Stimme (voice) counts. 'Einstimmig' (unanimous) means all voices agree. German democratic culture at the meeting-room level.
Feminine noun. Arbeit (work) + Gruppe (group).\nAbbreviated 'AG.' Each AG reports back to the Plenum.

Arbeit (work) + Gruppe (group). German project culture divides big tasks into Arbeitsgruppen. 'AG' for short. Each AG focuses on one aspect and reports back to the Plenum. Divide and conquer, German organizational style.
Neuter noun. From Latin plenum (full).\n'Im Plenum besprechen' means the whole group discusses.

From Latin 'plenum' (full assembly). After Arbeitsgruppen work separately, results return to the Plenum for collective decision. 'Im Plenum besprechen' (to discuss in the full group) is where decisions are made.
Neuter noun. Nominalized infinitive of netzwerken.\n'Beim Netzwerken' happens at every conference.

Netz (net) + Werk (work). The nominalized infinitive. Germans borrowed the concept from English but made it a neuter noun. 'Beim Netzwerken' (while networking) happens at every conference Rahmenprogramm. Kaffee und Kontakte.
Masculine noun. English loanword.\nMasculine because German Kurs (course) is masculine.

English loanword. Masculine because German 'Kurs' and 'Lehrgang' (course) are masculine. More hands-on than a Vortrag. 'Einen Workshop leiten' (to lead a workshop) means creating an interactive format. No lectures, only collaboration.
Regular verb. Not separable.\n'Eine Sitzung leiten' (to chair a meeting).

To lead, to direct, to conduct. 'Eine Sitzung leiten' (to chair a meeting). Not separable. Der Leiter/die Leiterin = the leader. 'Leitfaden' (guideline) shares the root. In German meetings, someone must always leiten.
Not separable. From Latin moderare (to regulate).\n'Eine Diskussion moderieren.'

From Latin 'moderare' (to regulate). Not separable. The Moderator moderiert: guiding the conversation without dominating it. In German TV, the Moderator is the host. In meetings, moderieren means keeping things on track and fair.
Separable: ein + beziehen. Wir beziehen alle ein.\nStrong verb: bezog ein, einbezogen.

Ein (in) + beziehen (to include/draw). Separable. Drawing people into the process. 'Alle einbeziehen' is the German ideal of inclusive decision-making. Nobody should be left outside the circle of consultation.
Separable: bei + tragen. Jeder trägt bei.\nStrong verb: trug bei, beigetragen.

Bei (alongside) + tragen (to carry). Separable. Carrying your share alongside others. Strong verb: beitragen/trug bei/beigetragen. 'Jeder trägt zum Erfolg bei' (Everyone contributes to the success). Teamwork, German style.
Separable: auf + greifen. Ich greife den Punkt auf.\nStrong verb: griff auf, aufgegriffen.
Auf (up) + greifen (to grab). Separable. Grabbing an idea and running with it. 'Ich greife Ihren Punkt auf' is essential German meeting rhetoric. It shows you listened and value the contribution.
Not separable. From Thema (topic).\nMore formal than ansprechen.

From 'Thema' (topic). Making something a Thema. Not separable. More formal than 'ansprechen.' 'Wir sollten das thematisieren' (We should address that) signals that something deserves the group's attention.
Not separable. Ver- + einbar (agreeable).\n'Wir vereinbaren den nächssten Termin.'

Ver- + einbar (agreeable). Not separable. Reaching a formal agreement. 'Wir vereinbaren den nächssten Termin' (We agree on the next date). 'Vereinbarung' (agreement) is the noun form. German meetings end with vereinbarte Schritte.
Not separable. Ver- + handeln (to act/trade).\nFrom Hand: negotiation is a handshake process.

Ver- + handeln (to act/trade). Not separable. From 'Hand' (hand): negotiation is a handshake process. 'Verhandlungsgeschick' (negotiation skill) is prized in German business culture. Every Kompromiss requires Verhandlung.
Not separable. Über (over) + zeugen (to generate).\n'Er überzeugte das Publikum.'

Über (over) + zeugen (to generate/witness). Not separable. Generating belief over someone. 'Überzeugend' (convincing) is the present participle. Verumius's presentation succeeded because it überzeugte through vivid examples, not just data.
Separable: zusammen + fassen. Ich fasse zusammen.\n'Zusammenfassung' is the noun: summary.

Zusammen (together) + fassen (to grasp). Separable. Grasping everything together into one statement. 'Zusammenfassung' is the noun: summary. 'Zusammenfassend lässt sich sagen...' (In summary, one can say...) is the classic German presentation closing.
Feminine noun. Teilnehmer (participant) + Liste.\nWho was present? The Protokoll needs this.

Teilnehmer (participant) + Liste (list). At German events, you sign the Teilnehmerliste at arrival. For internal meetings, it documents who was present for the Protokoll. No list, no proof you were there.
Neuter noun. These (thesis) + Papier (paper).\nA paper stating your arguments for discussion.

These (thesis) + Papier (paper). In German universities, you present your Thesenpapier before a seminar discussion. It states your arguments clearly for others to critique. A Thesenpapier is a commitment: here is what I believe and why.
Feminine noun. Tisch (table) + Vorlage (template/document).\nDistributed at the table during a meeting.

Tisch (table) + Vorlage (template/document). A document distributed at the table during a meeting. Literally 'table submission.' Given out at the start so participants can follow along. German meeting Ordnung at its most organized.
Neuter noun. Kern (core) + Thema (topic).\n'Was ist das Kernthema der Sitzung?'

Kern (core) + Thema (topic). The central question that everything revolves around. 'Was ist das Kernthema der Sitzung?' (What is the core topic of the meeting?). If you lose the Kernthema, you lose the meeting.
Masculine noun. Grund (ground/basic) + Gedanke (thought).\nThe bedrock of any argument.

Grund (ground/basic) + Gedanke (thought). The foundational thought. 'Der Grundgedanke meines Vortrags ist...' (The fundamental idea of my presentation is...). The bedrock upon which all other arguments are built.
Feminine noun. Geschäft (business) + Ordnung (order/rules).\nThe rulebook governing how meetings work.

Geschäft (business) + Ordnung (order/rules). Every German organization has one. It governs how meetings run, how votes work, and who may speak. 'Antrag zur Geschäftsordnung' (procedural motion) is the nuclear option in German meetings.
Aussage (statement) + kraeftig (strong).\n'Aussagekraeftige Daten' tell a clear story.
Aussage (statement) + kraeftig (strong). Data that tells a clear story. Strong enough to make a statement on its own. 'Aussagekraeftige Daten' is the gold standard in German project reporting.
Überblick (overview) + artig (like).\nThe bird's-eye view without detail.

Überblick (overview) + artig (like). Giving the big picture without too much detail. The bird's-eye view. 'Überblicksartig darstellen' (to present in overview form) is often the first step before diving into details.
Zu (to) + treffend (hitting).\n'Zutreffendes bitte ankreuzen' appears on every German form.
Zu (to) + treffend (hitting). Something that 'hits' the truth. 'Zutreffendes bitte ankreuzen' (Please check what applies) appears on every German form. Precise and correct.
An (at) + gemessen (measured).\n'Ein angemessener Ton' is the professional standard.
An (at) + gemessen (measured). Measured against what fits. The German standard for professional behavior. 'Angemessen reagieren' (to react appropriately) means finding exactly the right level.
Present participle of zusammenfassen.\n'Zusammenfassend lässt sich sagen...' closes presentations.

Present participle of 'zusammenfassen.' 'Zusammenfassend lässt sich sagen...' (In summary, one can say...) is the classic German presentation closing line. The present participle used as adverb. Professional and elegant.
Present participle of abschließen.\n'Abschließend möchte ich betonen...' is the final word.

Present participle of 'abschließen.' 'Abschließend möchte ich betonen...' (In closing, I would like to emphasize...). The final word before questions. More formal than 'schließlich.' It signals: the end is here.
Daran (to that) + anknüpfend (connecting).\nLinking your point to the previous speaker's idea.

Daran (to that) + anknüpfend (connecting). Linking your point to the previous speaker's contribution. Elegant meeting rhetoric. It shows you listened and are continuing the thread, not starting a new one.
Dies (this) + bezüglich (regarding).\nOne word replacing 'regarding this matter.'

Dies (this) + bezüglich (regarding). One word that replaces 'regarding this matter.' Compact and formal. 'Diesbezüglich habe ich eine Frage' (Regarding this, I have a question). German efficiency in a single word.
Eben (just/exactly) + so (so/thus).\n'Ebenso wichtig ist...' puts two things on the same level.

Eben (just/exactly) + so (so/thus). A transition word that puts two things on the same level. Short and effective. 'Ebenso wichtig ist...' (Equally important is...) is a common discourse marker in German presentations.
Separable: ab + schliessen.\nStrong verb: schloss ab, abgeschlossen.

Ab (off/away) + schliessen (to close). Separable. Closing something off definitively. Strong verb: abschließen/schloss ab/abgeschlossen. 'Eine Vereinbarung abschließen' (to finalize an agreement) is the goal of every Verhandlung.
Not separable. From Middle High German erliutern.\n'Können Sie das näher erläutern?'

From Middle High German 'erliutern' (to make clear). Not separable. More thorough than 'erklären.' 'Können Sie das näher erläutern?' (Can you explain that in more detail?) is one of the most frequent questions in German meetings.
Not separable. From Latin demonstrare.\nAlso means 'to protest' in a different context.

From Latin 'demonstrare.' Same word internationally. In business: 'Ich demonstriere die Funktion' (I demonstrate the function). But also: 'Für den Frieden demonstrieren' (to protest for peace). Context determines the meaning.
Separable: an + fuegen. Darf ich etwas anfügen?\nMore formal than hinzufügen.
An (at/on) + fuegen (to join/add). Separable. Joining something onto what already exists. 'Darf ich etwas anfügen?' (May I add something?) is formal and polite. More elegant than 'hinzufügen.'
Separable: bei + fuegen. Ich fuege das Dokument bei.\nStandard in German email culture.

Bei (alongside) + fuegen (to join). Separable. Adding something alongside the main item. 'Anbei fuege ich bei...' (Enclosed herewith...) is standard German email language. Every attachment is beigefuegt.
Polite presentation phrase.\nThe diplomatic way to draw attention to something.

The polite way to draw attention to something in a presentation. More diplomatic than 'Achtung!' or 'Bitte beachten Sie.' Standard Vortrag rhetoric that signals respect while delivering potentially unwelcome news.
Extremely formal. From gestatten (to permit).\nUsed to introduce a point or politely interrupt.

From 'gestatten' (to permit). Extremely formal. Royal court formality surviving in boardrooms. 'Gestatten Sie mir eine Bemerkung' (Allow me a remark) belongs to the highest register of German professional language.
Semi-formal. Softer than 'Gestatten Sie mir.'\nWorks in formal and semi-formal settings.

Softer than 'Gestatten Sie mir.' Works in formal and semi-formal settings. 'Wenn ich darf, möchte ich ein Beispiel anfügen.' A polite bridge before making your point. Not as stiff as Gestatten but still respectful.
Konjunktiv II of dürfen + bitten.\nThe most polite request formula in German.

Konjunktiv II of dürfen + bitten. The most polite request formula in German. 'Dürfte ich Sie bitten, die Unterlagen zu senden?' is more courteous than 'Können Sie bitte...' Subjunctive mood signals maximum politeness.
Konjunktiv II of sein + freundlich.\nOld-fashioned elegance for formal requests.
Konjunktiv II of sein + freundlich. Old-fashioned elegance still used in formal emails and presentations. 'Wären Sie so freundlich, das Mikrofon weiterzugeben?' is pure Höflichkeit at its finest.
Konjunktiv II expressing preference.\nUnderstated. The German way of saying 'I would prefer.'

Konjunktiv II expressing preference without imposition. 'Es wäre mir recht, wenn wir um 14 Uhr beginnen.' Understated. You do not demand. You express what would be recht (right/agreeable). Maximum politeness through minimal pressure.
Einhaken means 'to hook in.'\nThe polite way to interrupt during a discussion.

Einhaken literally means 'to hook in.' You hook your point into the conversation. The polite way to interrupt during a discussion. Essential for German meetings where cutting in rudely is unprofessional.
Standard transition phrase in German presentations.\n'Nun' signals forward movement.

The standard transition phrase in German presentations. 'Nun' (now) signals movement forward. Every structured Vortrag uses this between Tagesordnungspunkte. It tells the audience: one section ends, the next begins.
Instrumental subordinating conjunction.\nDescribes HOW something is done.

In + dem (the). Instrumental subordinate conjunction. The clause after 'indem' describes HOW something is done. 'Er überzeugte das Publikum, indem er Praxisbeispiele nutzte' (He convinced the audience by using practical examples).
Two-word connector. Dadurch (through that) + dass.\nMore explicit than indem.

Dadurch (through that) + dass. More explicit than 'indem.' Emphasizes the means or cause. Two-word connector introducing a subordinate clause. 'Dadurch, dass er gut vorbereitet war, überzeugte er' puts the cause front and center.
Genitive preposition. Mittel (means) + -s.\nFormal and precise. Used in technical writing.

Mittel (means) + -s (genitive). Genitive preposition. Formal and precise. 'Mittels eines Diagramms' (by means of a diagram). Used in technical and legal writing. The compact alternative to 'mit Hilfe von.'
Takes Dativ via 'von.'\nSlightly less formal than mittels.

Mit (with) + Hilfe (help) + von (of). Takes Dativ via 'von.' Slightly less formal than 'mittels.' 'Mithilfe von Praxisbeispielen' (with the help of practical examples) is everyday professional language.
Adjectival noun. Vor (before) + Sitzende (one who sits).\nDeclines like an adjective: der/die Vorsitzende.

Vor (before) + Sitzende (one who sits). The one who sits at the front. Declines like an adjective: der Vorsitzende (m), die Vorsitzende (f), des Vorsitzenden (gen). Gender-flexible by grammar. Every Verein, Ausschuss, and Partei has one.
Separable: aus + führen.\nDouble meaning: execute or explain in detail.
Aus (out) + führen (to lead). Separable. Double meaning: execute ('den Plan ausführen') or explain in detail ('Wie ich bereits ausgeführt habe'). Leading something out from the abstract into reality.
Not separable. Unter (under) + breiten (to spread).\n'Ich unterbreite Ihnen einen Vorschlag.'

Unter (under) + breiten (to spread). Not separable. Spreading your idea under someone for their consideration. Very formal. 'Ich unterbreite Ihnen einen Vorschlag' is boardroom language at its most elegant.
Separable: aus + arbeiten.\nWorking something from raw idea to finished product.
Aus (out) + arbeiten (to work). Separable. Working something out from raw idea to finished product. 'Ausgearbeitet' = fully developed. A rough idea becomes a concrete plan through Ausarbeitung.
Separable: ein + führen.\n'Wir führen ein neues System ein.'

Ein (in) + führen (to lead). Separable. Leading something new into existence. 'Einführung' = introduction (of a system, product, or concept). Not to be confused with 'vorstellen' (to introduce a person).
Separable: zusammen + tragen.\nStrong verb: trug zusammen, zusammengetragen.
Zusammen (together) + tragen (to carry). Separable. Carrying pieces together into one place. Strong verb: zusammentragen/trug zusammen/zusammengetragen. The gathering phase before analysis.
Not separable. Ver- + weisen (to point).\n'Ich verweise auf die Studie.'
Ver- + weisen (to point/show). Not separable. Pointing someone toward a source. 'Verweis' (reference) is the noun. Essential in academic and professional presentations. Every claim needs a Verweis.
Separable: an + deuten.\nSubtle and diplomatic. 'Andeutung' is the noun.

An (at) + deuten (to interpret/point). Separable. Not stating directly but pointing toward. Subtle and diplomatic. 'Andeutung' (hint) is the noun. In German business, sometimes people deuten an rather than state directly.
Separable: über + leiten.\nLeading the audience from one topic to the next.

Über (over) + leiten (to lead). Separable. Leading the audience over from one topic to the next. The art of smooth Überleitungen separates good speakers from great ones. A clumsy Überleitung loses the audience.
Separable: vor + stellen.\nAlso reflexive: sich vorstellen (to introduce oneself).
Vor (before) + stellen (to place). Separable. Placing something before the audience. Also reflexive: 'Ich stelle mich vor' (I introduce myself). The most versatile presentation verb in German.
Multi-word verb. Gegeneinander (against each other) + abwägen.\nThe core of German deliberative culture.

Gegeneinander (against each other) + abwägen (to weigh). Putting arguments on a scale. The core of German deliberative culture. No decision is made without careful Abwägung. Pros on one side, cons on the other, then decide.
